items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.correct: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to INCREASE a behaviour.
items.misconception: Punishing a behaviour to decrease it.
items.random_wrong: Giving a reward to decrease behaviour.
items.why: Students confuse negative reinforcement with punishment. Both involve something unpleasant, but reinforcement (positive or negative) always INCREASES behaviour; punishment decreases it.

items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.

items.q: The brain and spinal cord together form the:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: CNS. “PNS” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Fight-or-flight is triggered by which nervous system branch?
items.why: Correct answer: Sympathetic. “Parasympathetic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many lobes does each cerebral hemisphere have?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 lobes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
